records.ety: [AS. faran to travel, fare; akin to OS., Goth., & OHG. faran to travel, go, D. varen, G. fahren, OFries., Icel., & Sw. fara, Dan. fare, Gr. <?/<?/<?/<?/<?/ a way through, <?/<?/<?/<?/<?/<?/<?/ a ferry, strait, <?/<?/<?/<?/<?/<?/<?/<?/ to convey, <?/<?/<?/<?/<?/<?/<?/<?/<?/<?/ to go, march, <?/<?/<?/<?/<?/ beyond, on the other side, <?/<?/<?/<?/<?/ to pass through, L. peritus experienced, portus port, Skr. par to bring over. √78. Cf. Chaffer, Emporium, Far, Ferry, Ford, Peril, Port a harbor, Pore, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Fared (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Faring.]

defs: To go; to pass; to journey; to travel.
quotes: So on he fares, and to the border comes Of Eden.

defs: To be in any state, or pass through any experience, good or bad; to be attended with any circummstances or train of events, fortunate or unfortunate; as, he fared well, or ill.
quotes: So fares the stag among the enraged hounds.
I bid you most heartily well to fare.
So fared the knight between two foes.

defs: To be treated or entertained at table, or with bodily or social comforts; to live.
quotes: There was a certain rich man which . . . fared sumptuously every day.

defs: To happen well, or ill; -- used impersonally; as, we shall see how it will fare with him.
quotes: So fares it when with truth falsehood contends.

defs: To behave; to conduct one's self.
quotes: She ferde [fared] as she would die.

records.ety: [AS. faru journey, fr. faran. See Fare, v.]

quotes: That nought might stay his fare.

defs: The price of passage or going; the sum paid or due for conveying a person by land or water; as, the fare for crossing a river; the fare in a coach or by railway.

quotes: The warder chid and made fare.

defs: Condition or state of things; fortune; hap; cheer.
Food; provisions for the table; entertainment; as, coarse fare; delicious fare.
The person or persons conveyed in a vehicle; as, a full fare of passengers.
The catch of fish on a fishing vessel.
